methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $2.0B last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $500M - $5.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 25.3x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: Etched
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $5B

private.name: Substrate
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $1B+

private.name: Rain AI
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $500M

data.narrative:
d-Matrix is a Series A chip design company valued at $2.0B post-money, having raised $450M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparable universe of 3 public semiconductor players (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) and 8 private frontier-chip companies, with closest stage peers including Etched ($5.0B), Substrate ($1.0B), and Rain AI ($500M).

Two triangulation methods apply. Stage-progression analysis applies a typical 2.0x step-up multiplier to the $2.0B last post-money valuation, implying $4.0B. Peer-median benchmarking across the 3 same-sector, same-stage comparables yields a median valuation of $1.0B, with a range of $500M to $5.0B. Public-comp anchoring via price-to-sales multiples (median 25.3x across the semiconductor peer set) cannot be applied due to lack of disclosed revenue data for the target.

The two applicable methods triangulate to a valuation range of $850M to $4.6B, with a median implied value of $2.5B. The current $2.0B valuation sits within this range but closer to the lower-median bound. Key uncertainties include the timing and composition of the next funding round, which would typically reset stage multiples, and the extent to which d-Matrix's specific product differentiation justifies positioning relative to the wide $500M–$5.0B peer dispersion. Revenue trajectory and customer traction remain unobservable from available data.
